Docker provides users the ability to define minimal specifications of environments meaning you can easily develop, ship, and scale applications. Why should the body’s inflammatory response, which is supposed to be on our side in the battle against disease, be causing us to feel depressed?

context.title: How to Give a Perfect Answer
context.chunk_preview:
Remember that a question is a demand.

My favorite hero in this connection is Robert Goren of “Law and Order: Criminal Intent.”

Goren’s job is to interrogate people. But he doesn’t pepper the suspect with questions. He is selective.

He goes into the interrogation already knowing who did the crime. He doesn’t ask questions about what he needs to know. He asks questions about what the other person needs to go on record as saying.

It’s very crafty.

Goren understands that you can either close people down or open them up, depending on what kind of question you ask.

Finally, don’t ask rhetorical questions.

For a variety of reasons, these are unhelpful. A rhetorical question tends to sound sarcastic, and that’s hard to pull off in writing.

The reader doesn’t have much information to go on.

(He may even think your rhetorical question is sincere, in which case he will question your intelligence.)

Rhetorical questions can be interpreted multiple ways, so they are the opposite of clarity.

Be plain. Be clear. Be brief.

Don’t make it demanding.

Mistake #4: Don’t Make It Rude

question: Summarize the main idea of the article 'Thinking About Software Sustainably'.
expected_answer: The main idea is that software sustainability involves not just technical but also economic, environmental, and social perspectives, and that developers should think about building software to endure change from these four angles.
evidence_quote: Various academic papers have actually identified four perspectives for sustainable software: Economic sustainability, Environmental sustainability, Social sustainability, Technical sustainability
actual_answer:
Main idea: The article argues that “sustainability” for software means designing systems to endure and adapt to change, not just reducing environmental impact. It says sustainability is “about the capacity to endure” and cites Becker et al.: “Sustainability of a system describes how well the system will continue to exist and function, even as circumstances may change.” The piece points out that developers already pursue this through maintainability, scalability and other “‑ilities,” but urges thinking about sustainability across four perspectives—economic, environmental, social, and technical—so software can better withstand changing circumstances.

Explanation using the article: the author frames sustainability beyond environmental buzzwords (passage: “Sustainability is not just about preserving the environment…sustainability is about the capacity to endure”), defines the goal as handling change (quote from Becker et al.), links this goal to familiar engineering aims (maintainability, scalability), and lists the four academic perspectives for software-related sustainability.

title: How Human Breastmilk Can Block Coronavirus’s Replication
question: What is the central idea of the article 'How Human Breastmilk Can Block Coronavirus’s Replication'?
expected_answer: The core idea is that human breastmilk, specifically whey proteins like lactoferrin, can block SARS-CoV-2 replication in vitro by preventing viral entry and inhibiting RNA polymerase, supporting the safety of breastfeeding during Covid-19.
evidence_quote: human breastmilk inhibits not only viral entry but also viral replication
actual_answer: The central idea is that a recent laboratory study found skimmed human breastmilk — specifically its heat-sensitive proteins such as lactoferrin — strongly blocks replication of SARS‑CoV‑2 and a related pangolin coronavirus in cultured cells. The article summarizes the study’s main findings: skimmed breastmilk reduced viral replication by about 98%, the antiviral activity was lost after heating (indicating proteins are responsible), and human whey (rich in lactoferrin) was more potent than cow or goat whey. The piece uses these results to argue that Covid‑19 alone is not a good reason to avoid breastfeeding. I base this on the article’s account: “skimmed breastmilk blocked the replication of both SARS‑CoV‑2 and its relative pangolin coronavirus by 98%,” heated samples “lost their antiviral effects,” and “human milk is rich in LF [lactoferrin], which is 10–100 fold higher than that in cow and goat milk.”

By the time of my root canal surgery, in 2013, the questions were being asked much more often, and more precisely, and the answers were becoming clearer, thanks to the work of a disruptive new science, which has continued to make rapid progress in the past five years.

Why should the body’s inflammatory response, which is supposed to be on our side in the battle against disease, be causing us to feel depressed?

It exists at the boundaries between immunology, neuroscience, psychology, and psychiatry. Much later evolved the more complex, but still single celled organisms called eukaryotes. From those humble beginnings came the multi-cellular life forms called metazoans. All animal cells, including humans, are eukaryotic cells. Since they share a common origin, they bear a resemblance to each other. Many molecular mechanisms (genes, enzymes, etc.) and biochemical pathways are conserved throughout the evolution towards more complex organisms.

title: Seth Godin’s Rerouted Flight Changed My Outlook on Marketing
question: In your own words, what is the main message of 'Seth Godin’s Rerouted Flight Changed My Outlook on Marketing'?
expected_answer: The main message is that marketing is about giving people what they want and reminding them of what they already desire, not about pushing products on them. The story of Seth Godin's failed attempt to offer a ride illustrates that no one wants to be sold, but everyone wants to buy.
evidence_quote: Marketing isn’t pushing crap into people’s hands. It’s reminding them what they always wanted in the first place.
actual_answer: The article’s main message is that marketing should help people get what they already want rather than force something on them. The author contrasts marketing’s power to grow a business with a stronger point: “what’s even better than growth and commerce? Giving people what they want.” He uses Seth Godin’s rerouted-flight anecdote—Godin offering free car seats and getting “NONE!”—to illustrate that people resist being sold to (“everyone is looking to buy, but no one wants to be sold”). As the author puts it, “Marketing isn’t pushing crap into people’s hands. It’s reminding them what they always wanted in the first place,” and there is honor in honestly convincing the right people.

The best intervention may actually be to respond from a detached perspective — almost as if you were another person.

This is called self-distancing.

To self-distance, one replaces the first-person pronoun I with a non-first-person pronoun, you or he/she, when talking to themselves.

Example:

“I didn’t want to make an emotional decision. I wanted to do what was best for LeBron James and what would make him happy.” — Basketball player LeBron James describing his decision to leave his old team.

Essentially, when people referred to themselves in the second-person or using their own name, they were able to improve their ability to detach emotionally from the situation.

The theory behind this is that people tend to display high levels of wise reasoning when they give advice to others, but not when they decide how to act themselves.

question: Summarize the key idea of the article 'Astronomers Spot Comet Chury’s Ultraviolet Aurora'.
expected_answer: The key idea is that researchers using data from the Rosetta mission have for the first time identified an ultraviolet aurora around comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko, caused by solar wind electrons interacting with the comet's coma, providing a new way to study solar wind without the need for a spacecraft on site.

actual_answer: Key idea: Using data from ESA’s Rosetta mission, researchers (led from the University of Bern) have for the first time identified an ultraviolet aurora around comet 67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko. The UV glow is produced when solar-wind electrons are accelerated toward the comet and strike its coma, exciting hydrogen and oxygen atoms that then emit ultraviolet light — a process similar in some ways to planetary auroras but distinct from ordinary “airglow.” The discovery (published in Nature Astronomy) was possible by combining Rosetta’s in situ and remote-sensing instruments (RPC, ROSINA, VIRTIS, MIRO and Alice), and corrects earlier misattribution of the UV emission as sunlight/airglow. (From “Astronomers Spot Comet Chury’s Ultraviolet Aurora,” Robert Lea.)

One of the most fascinating theories regarding space is that of the Kardashev scale. Meant to describe what alien civilizations would look like, it labels our own in comparison to what may be in the cosmos.

What is the Kardashev Scale?

Proposed originally by Russian astrophysicist, Nikolai Kardashev, this scale measures potential civilizations by energy output in 3 stages.

He believed that a civilization is measured on a cosmic level by its’ energy usage and the technology it uses. According to Kardashev, these two aspects ran parallel to one another.

As more energy is produced, higher levels of technology are needed to produce it. Therefore, a society that has a high energy output must have matching technology.

In other words, more energy output = more technologically advanced.

Here’s the sad part…humans aren’t even on this scale yet.

While they technically make some profits by earning interest and rebates, the bulk >40% of their revenue comes from re-routing and allowing high frequency firms to operate their own pools to take advantage of unwitting customers.

Logistically, there has to be a reason that HFTs pay for this kind of market control. Partially, this comes from arbitrage of purchasing from public markets at the lowest price and marking up to Robinhood customers. This is ostensibly to provide “liquidity” or “make markets.” But in reality, this is a tax on Robinhood traders.

But an even more nefarious reason that HFTs want this level of access is to take the other side of a trade against Robinhood customers. Since most traders are unsophisticated and lose money (according to most estimates, 80% of day traders are unprofitable over the course of a year) market makers and HFTs will gladly take the opposite trade as the average Robinhood trader and will on average be right.

Passage:
There Are No Green Stars

When astrophysicists peer into the universe, they see a nearly perfect rainbow spectrum of stars, from cooler red ones on one side to hotter blue ones on the other. In the center of the spectrum, they should see green stars, but they don’t due to the nature of stars and the limitations of the human eye.
